summary: Crisp soundboard recording captures a top‑tier 1994 Boston show highlighted by a smoking Help > Slip > Franklin’s, a searing Scarlet → Fire transition and an emotional So Many Roads.
review: The Bertha‐sourced soundboard is clear and well balanced, letting the band’s tight jamming shine through.  Highlights include the fiery Help‑Slip‑Franklin’s sequence, a legendary Scarlet → Fire segue, and Jerry’s passionate vocal on So Many Roads, with strong support from Vince, Phil, Bob and the twin‑drum attack.  While a few dissenting voices call the era “declining,” the overwhelming consensus is that this is one of the finest post‑Brent performances.

ai_rating.rationale: The majority of reviews (≈70 %) are 4–5 stars praising both the musical performance and the clear soundboard recording; only a handful of low‑star outliers detract minimally, yielding an overall rating in the good‑to‑excellent range.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Crisp, clear mix with excellent instrument separation; some reviewers note it is cleaner than other SBD copies, no major dropouts or muddiness reported.

show_quality.setlist_flow: First set tight and energetic, second set flows superbly with standout transitions (Help‑Slip‑Franklin’s, Scarlet‑Fire) and a powerful closing sequence.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Vocals on So Many Roads praised as emotional and powerful; guitar work includes memorable MIDI flute during Scarlet → Fire and gritty tone on Slip.
band_member_comments.Phil: Bass line on the Scarlet → Fire transition highlighted as ripping and tightly locked with Jerry's riffs.
band_member_comments.Bob: Generally noted for staying in the pocket and supporting the band without over‑playing.
band_member_comments.Keys: Vince’s playing described as tasteful, clean in the mix, and “invincibly convincing” throughout.
band_member_comments.Drums: Bill and Mickey receive praise for high energy, excellent cymbal work, and a standout Terrapin → Jam → Drums segment.
